---
title: "Exploring Economic Mobility through Data"
author: "Andrew Doherty-Munro and Sabrina Beaver
format:
  pdf:
include-in-header:
      text: |
        % Enable wrapping for Pandoc code blocks in PDF
        \usepackage{fvextra}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{makecell}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \DefineVerbatimEnvironment{Highlighting}{Verbatim}{breaklines,commandchars=\\\{\}}
editor: source
execute-dir: project
execute: 
    echo: true
---
Group Members: Andrew Doherty-Munro and Sabrina Beaver
Github Usernames: adohertymunro and sabtriestech
Section: Monday 10:30 (Section 2)

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
# setup
import geopandas as gpd
from pathlib import Path
import os
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib as mpl

BASE_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent
BASE_DIR
os.chdir(BASE_DIR)
```

# Research Question

Our project aimed to explore the social and economic factors that are related to high- and low-mobility counties. In brief: What contributes to areas where children do better than their parents? To do this we brout together Opportunity Insights Causal Place Data, QCEW data on industry concentration, FBI data on crime, and U.S. Census data for population and shapefiles.

# Data Approach

In terms of county coverage, crime statistics were the limiting factor. Since the Opportunity Insights data focuses on those that moved from 1996-2012, we needed to find some overlapping data for crime in this period. The best source availaible was from county level data from the Crime in the US Report. Data cleaning began by downloading yearly tables, stripping them of unnecessary headers and footnotes, concatenating different years, and harmonizing the names of counties to allow merging with our other data sources. Since the mobility indicators cover a range of years, we averaged crime for the overlapping period and converted it to a rate per 100,000 people to make it more comparable between counties with vastly different populations. There were a few counties that were significant outliers in terms of crime rates. We winsorized these observations to the 95th percentile. 

# Difficulties

One difficulty we had resulted from the limited coverage in crime data. Since reporting is voluntary, there were entire states missing from our crime data. More importantly, some of these missing states, such as Massachusetts, are near the top of income and education distribution, so the resulting picture of the U.S. that we get may be missing important variation. 

# Resulting Static Plots

The first four static plots are geospatial visualizations of different crime and mobility measures, as we wanted to visualize the discrepancies between regions. Maps for both property and violent crime show a significantly higher crime rates in the Southeast U.S. than in other regions. However, due to methodology behind the mobility data, there was much less visible variation with the exception of a few outliers. For this reason, we decided to not visualize mobility in our dashboard, but to have it as filter to explore other variables. These 4 visualizations highlighted that there wasn't much visible overlap between our crime and mobility measures, contrary to our expectations. 

```{python}
#| echo: false
data_w_geo= gpd.read_file(os.path.join("data\derived_data\Full Data with Geography.gpkg"))

county_map = gpd.read_file(os.path.join('data\Shapefiles\County\co99_d00.shp'))
#drop Alaska and Hawaii
county_map = county_map[county_map['STATE'] != '02']
county_map = county_map[county_map['STATE'] != '15']

#want to collapse to single row per county
data_grouped_geo = data_w_geo[
    (data_w_geo["Ownership"] == "Private") &
    (data_w_geo["Industry"] == "Total, all industries")]

data_grouped_geo = data_grouped_geo[data_grouped_geo["state_name"]!="Hawaii"]

#want to winsorize property crime to display better
pctile95 = data_grouped_geo['Property crime_rate'].quantile(0.95)
data_grouped_geo["Property crime_rate_winsor"] = np.where(data_grouped_geo["Property crime_rate"] > pctile95, 
                                                     pctile95, 
                                                     data_grouped_geo["Property crime_rate"]) 

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(12, 8))

county_map.plot(
    color='lightgrey',   
    ax=ax)

data_grouped_geo.plot(
    column='Property crime_rate_winsor',              
    linewidth=0.2,           
    edgecolor='0.5',          
    legend=True,              
    ax=ax,
    zorder=2,
    cmap='magma_r'
    )

cbar_ax = fig.axes[-1]
cbar_ax.set_title('Property Crime Rate\n(per 100,000 residents)', fontsize=10)
ax.set_position([0.03, 0.08, 0.82, 0.85])
cbar_ax.set_position([0.87, 0.25, 0.02, 0.5])

ax.set_title('County-Level Property Crime Rates', fontsize=24)
ax.axis('off')  

ax.set_axis_off()
plt.show()
```


```{python}
#| echo: false
pctile95 = data_grouped_geo['Violent crime_rate'].quantile(0.95)
data_grouped_geo["Violent crime_rate_winsor"] = np.where(data_grouped_geo["Violent crime_rate"] > pctile95, 
                                                     pctile95, 
                                                     data_grouped_geo["Violent crime_rate"]) 

# now for violent crime
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(12, 8))

county_map.plot(
    color='lightgrey',   
    ax=ax)

data_grouped_geo.plot(
    column='Violent crime_rate_winsor',              
    linewidth=0.2,           
    edgecolor='0.5',          
    legend=True,              
    ax=ax,
    zorder=2,
    cmap='magma_r',
    )

cbar_ax = fig.axes[-1]
cbar_ax.set_title('Violent Crime Rate\n(per 100,000 residents)', fontsize=10)
ax.set_position([0.03, 0.08, 0.82, 0.85])
cbar_ax.set_position([0.87, 0.25, 0.02, 0.5])

ax.set_title('County-Level Violent Crime Rates', fontsize=24)
ax.axis('off')  

ax.set_axis_off()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(12, 8))

county_map.plot(
    color='lightgrey',   
    ax=ax)

norm = mpl.colors.Normalize(vmin=-9, vmax=7)
data_grouped_geo.plot(
    column='kr26_p25_coef',              
    linewidth=0.2,           
    edgecolor='0.5',          
    legend=True,              
    ax=ax,
    zorder=2,
    cmap='magma_r',
    norm=norm
    )

cbar_ax = fig.axes[-1]
cbar_ax.set_title('Mobility Coefficient', fontsize=10)
ax.set_position([0.03, 0.08, 0.82, 0.85])
cbar_ax.set_position([0.87, 0.25, 0.02, 0.5])

ax.set_title('County-Level Mobility for 25th percentile', fontsize=24)
ax.axis('off')  

ax.set_axis_off()
plt.show()

```

```{python}
#| echo: false
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(12, 8))

county_map.plot(
    color='lightgrey',   
    ax=ax)

data_grouped_geo.plot(
    column='kr26_p75_coef',              
    linewidth=0.2,           
    edgecolor='0.5',          
    legend=True,              
    ax=ax,
    zorder=2,
    cmap='magma_r',
    )

cbar_ax = fig.axes[-1]
cbar_ax.set_title('Mobility Coefficient', fontsize=10)
ax.set_position([0.03, 0.08, 0.82, 0.85])
cbar_ax.set_position([0.87, 0.25, 0.02, 0.5])

ax.set_title('County-Level Mobility for 75th percentile', fontsize=24)
ax.axis('off')  

ax.set_axis_off()
plt.show()
```

# Streamlit App

Our Streamlit app contains two main components and a number of filters. First, it presents the user with a dropdown box with a number of indicators including crime statistics, HHI for industry concentration, and median housing prices for the 25th and 75th percentiles. Once the user selects an input, the left-hand side of the page presents a spatial representation of different variables, with a tooltip for users to expore county names and values for that indicator. 

The right-hand side of the webpage presents the viewer with a histogram of the selected variable and prints the mean value on the graph. This column has an additional filter that allows users to generate a histogram for counties above or below the median value in terms of mobility. While the mobility coefficients are centered around zero, and don't provide that much information, seeing how housing prices, crime, and industry concentration vary between high and low mobility counties. As discussed above, housing prices show a very strong correlation with mobility.
